key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr-rich BCC/Ni-rich FCC interface in an ultrafine eutectic enables synchronized plasticity, letting the hard phase deform by dislocations at high strength instead of fracturing early.
engineering_relevance: Interface chemistry and nanoscale spacing can be engineered in additively manufactured eutectics to improve ductility without sacrificing flow strength.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion produced a lamellar Cr-Ni eutectic with ~450 nm interlamellar spacing.
Atomic-resolution STEM/HR-STEM identified a Kurdjumov-Sachs stepped semi-coherent FCC/BCC interface and Ni enrichment near the BCC side.
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension, plus TEM correlation and atomistic modeling, tracked the deformation sequence and slip activation.
evidence_signal: In situ mechanical testing showed high flow stresses with large plastic strains and no cracking, supported by microstructural evidence of dislocation accumulation, twinning, interfacial shear, and later BCC dislocation mobility.
watch_item: The demonstrated co-deformation depends on precise interface structure and local chemistry, so scaling or processing deviations could reduce ductility and trigger brittle failure.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
